title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman lays out five interlocking reasons abolitionists reject incremental pro-life legislation: such bills are unbiblical, unconstitutional, dehumanizing, reinforce judicial supremacy, and serve as a substitute for the immediate and total abolition of abortion.
audiences: Pro-life Christian considering the abolitionist critique
State legislator weighing incremental versus abolition bills
Pastor or theologian evaluating the ethics of regulating abortion
Constitutional conservative concerned about judicial supremacy
Survivor or person conceived in rape engaging the abortion debate

questions.question: What is the abolitionist's primary objection to incremental pro-life bills?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian considering the abolitionist critique
questions.answer: Silberman frames the foundational objection as biblical: incremental bills explicitly codify into law that murder is permitted under certain circumstances, which violates God's commands against murder. Pastor C.R. Cali is cited to argue that regulating abortion legitimizes the practice rather than engendering abhorrence for it.
questions.quote: The rationale for opposing incremental pro-life bills is first and foremost that they are unbiblical. They violate God's commands against murder. By regulating abortion, they explicitly codify into law that murder is legal under certain circumstances.

questions.question: Why does Silberman call the incremental approach a form of moral relativism?
questions.audience: Pastor or theologian evaluating the ethics of regulating abortion
questions.answer: Because the same bill would be judged 'good' or 'bad' depending only on whether it improves on the local status quo, the morality of the law floats with circumstance rather than with a fixed standard. Silberman argues this is a relativistic and decidedly anti-Christian ethic.
questions.quote: From this perspective, a ban on abortion at 24 weeks would be a good or just law in California (because it's better than the California status quo) while simultaneously being a bad or unjust law in Oklahoma (because it's worse than the Oklahoma status quo). This is not a Christian approach to ethics. This is a relativistic and decidedly anti-Christian approach to ethics.

questions.question: How does the U.S. Constitution figure into the case against incremental bills?
questions.audience: Constitutional conservative concerned about judicial supremacy
questions.answer: Silberman appeals to the 14th Amendment's equal protection clause to argue that any law outlawing the murder of some human beings while permitting the murder of others is itself unconstitutional. Supporting such a law means flouting the very foundations the case against abortion rests upon.
questions.quote: A law that outlaws the murder of certain human beings and allows the murder of others is a violation of the Constitution. So when we write or support a law that does that, we are not only giving up the foundations of the case against abortion (the Bible and the Constitution), we are flouting them.

questions.question: How do incremental pro-life bills allegedly dehumanize preborn children?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian considering the abolitionist critique
questions.answer: Silberman argues each incremental bill draws an arbitrary line (a heartbeat, pain capacity, gestational age, or rape exception) that teaches the public the children outside that line don't yet count as fully human. He gives concrete examples of a state senator's aide, an abortion-minded mother, and a college student each repeating dehumanizing premises learned directly from pro-life bills.
questions.quote: Incremental pro-life bills dehumanize those children who don't meet their arbitrary requirements for legal protection, specific to each bill.

questions.question: What does the rape-exception story say to people conceived in rape?
questions.audience: Survivor or person conceived in rape engaging the abortion debate
questions.answer: Silberman recounts a young woman conceived in rape who broke down crying when Sam Riley told her abortion is wrong even in cases of rape, because she had heard the rape exception as a message that her own life is worth less. Bills with rape exceptions, on this account, send that same message to every person conceived in rape.
questions.quote: "Thank you for saying that," she told him. "I was conceived in rape and when people talk about a rape exception for abortion, what I hear is that my life is less valuable." Not all pro-life bills have rape exceptions, but many of them do. These bills dehumanize those conceived in rape.

questions.question: How do incremental bills strengthen judicial supremacy?
questions.audience: Constitutional conservative concerned about judicial supremacy
questions.answer: Because incremental bills are written in explicit submission to Roe, they signal to the Supreme Court that the other branches will not challenge its authority. Silberman, drawing on Frederick Douglass, argues that power concedes nothing without a demand and that 47 years of submissive bills have produced no concessions, even from a Republican-dominated court.
questions.quote: Pro-life laws demand nothing. They're a beg for scraps. Pro-life laws are written explicitly in submission to Roe because pro-lifers don't think they can stand up to the courts. This further entrenches the idea that the court's authority is unassailable.

questions.question: What concrete remedies does Silberman propose for an out-of-control judiciary?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing incremental versus abolition bills
questions.answer: He names two constitutional checks the other branches and the states can use: nullification of evil and unconstitutional decisions, and impeachment of justices who defy the Constitution. Refusing to use either, he says, effectively grants the judiciary unlimited power.
questions.quote: There are two ways to check the power of the judiciary: nullification and impeachment. If we're unwilling to ignore evil, unconstitutional decisions and we're unwilling to impeach justices who defy the Constitution, then the judiciary has unlimited power.

questions.question: In what sense are incremental bills a 'substitute' for abolition?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing incremental versus abolition bills
questions.answer: Silberman points to the 2019-2020 Oklahoma sessions, where pro-life politicians refused to hear the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act and then pushed incremental bills to quell the resulting outrage. Echoing William Lloyd Garrison, he frames such schemes as substitutes that delay rather than deliver immediate and total abolition.
questions.quote: Incremental pro-life bills are being used by pro-life politicians as a **substitute for the immediate and total abolition of abortion**. Abortion will only be abolished when we take the option to compromise off the table for pro-life politicians.

questions.question: Why does Silberman think Christians' submission to the Supreme Court must have limits?
questions.audience: Pastor or theologian evaluating the ethics of regulating abortion
questions.answer: He cites Acts 5:29 to insist that Christians' only unconditional submission is to God; when obedience to God conflicts with obedience to man, God wins. He uses Texas Alliance for Life director Joe Pojman's claim that ignoring SCOTUS is like ignoring gravity to illustrate the anti-Christian ethic of unconditional submission to the courts.
questions.quote: This is anathema to the ideas and documents upon which the U.S. was founded, but more importantly, it's another decidedly anti-Christian ethic. Our only unconditional submission is to God, and when we're forced to choose between obedience to God or obedience to man, we obey God (Acts 5:29).

questions.question: What would actually force pro-life politicians to abolish abortion, according to Silberman?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian considering the abolitionist critique
questions.answer: Removing the option to compromise. As long as Christians tolerate incremental substitutes, politicians will keep offering them; the day Christians refuse to accept incremental bills as substitutes is the day abolition becomes politically possible in conservative states.
questions.quote: For as long as we tolerate compromise, they will compromise. The day pro-life politicians know that Christians will no longer tolerate incremental pro-life bills being used as substitutes, abortion will be abolished in conservative states.

related.title: WATCH: How the Seven-Pronged Pro-Life Political Strategy Has Failed
related.reason: Silberman's follow-up presentation expanding on why the incremental pro-life political strategy has failed to abolish abortion.

related.title: Why Do Pro-Lifers Despise Abolitionists?
related.reason: Same author tackles the institutional pro-life backlash to abolitionists who refuse incremental compromise, citing William Lloyd Garrison.

related.title: Pro-Life Idaho State Rep: "We all know nullification doesn't happen in reality."
related.reason: Concrete case study of the judicial-supremacy mindset Silberman attacks under reason #4.

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the "Villains" of American History?
related.reason: Defends nullification as a legitimate constitutional check, the remedy Silberman urges against the courts.

related.title: King Josiah's Smashmouth Abolitionism
related.reason: Biblical/theological case against incrementalism, complementing Silberman's first reason that incremental bills are unbiblical.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Distills the identity distinction Silberman is drawing between abolitionism and the pro-life establishment.

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: Primer on the nullification remedy Silberman names as one of two checks on a tyrannical judiciary.
